name: Governance, Risk & Compliance (GRC)

users.context: Maintains the control framework, maps regulations to controls, tracks attestations and policy acknowledgements, and reports compliance posture to leadership and regulators. Deadline- and evidence-driven.
users.expertise: Expert in regulatory frameworks and control design; high tooling fluency in GRC platforms.

users.context: Plans and executes audits, tests control effectiveness, samples evidence, documents findings, and tracks remediation. Read-mostly into operational systems, write into audit workpapers.
users.expertise: Expert in audit methodology and sampling; needs traceable evidence and immutable workpapers.

users.context: Maintains the risk register, scores likelihood/impact, defines treatments, monitors KRIs, and runs risk assessments across the enterprise.
users.expertise: Expert in risk frameworks (e.g., ISO 31000, COSO); works with heatmaps and registers.

users.context: Operates and attests to assigned controls, uploads evidence, and responds to findings. Compliance is secondary to their day job, so friction must be low.
users.expertise: Domain expert in their function, novice in GRC terminology; needs guided, low-burden tasks.

users.context: Maintains records of processing, runs DPIAs, handles data-subject requests (DSARs) and breach reporting under privacy law.
users.expertise: Expert in GDPR/CCPA and privacy operations.

users.context: Assesses third-party risk, collects security questionnaires and certifications, monitors ongoing vendor compliance.
users.expertise: Expert in TPRM and security frameworks (SOC 2, ISO 27001).

workflows.name: Control attestation and evidence collection
steps: Assign periodic control attestations to owners
Owner reviews control and uploads evidence

workflows.name: Internal audit lifecycle
steps: Plan audit scope and risk-based selection

workflows.name: Incident / breach and findings remediation
steps: Assess regulatory reporting obligation and clock

risks: Missing a regulatory reporting deadline (e.g., 72-hour breach notification) and incurring penalties
Attesting a control as effective without adequate evidence (false assurance)
Broken evidence trail so an audit finding cannot be substantiated
Evidence or workpaper altered after the fact, destroying audit integrity
Granting access that breaks the three-lines independence (auditor able to edit operational data)
Stale risk register hiding a materialised risk
Mis-mapped regulation-to-control leaving a requirement uncovered
Overdue remediation silently slipping past its due date
Sensitive evidence (PII, security findings) exposed to unauthorised users
Policy change not acknowledged by required population, leaving a control gap
environment_constraints: Evidence must be immutable, timestamped, and traceable for years
Three-lines-of-defence separation between operators, oversight, and audit
Cross-functional users with low GRC literacy (control owners)
High sensitivity of contents (security findings, PII, legal)
Periodic spikes around audit and reporting cycles
domain_patterns: Immutable, timestamped evidence and audit trail
Risk heatmap and register (likelihood x impact)
Workflow with due dates, owners, and escalation
Sampling and traceability from finding to source evidence
audit_compliance: GDPR / CCPA privacy (DPIA, DSAR, 72-hour breach notice)
Industry rules (HIPAA, PCI-DSS, Basel) as mapped
Records retention and legal-hold for evidence
accessibility_concerns: Risk heatmaps must not encode severity by colour alone (add labels/patterns/ranking)
Dense control matrices need screen-reader-friendly headers and navigation
Status and overdue states need text/icon, not colour only
Plain-language task framing for non-expert control owners
Keyboard-navigable evidence upload and attestation forms
Accessible exports (tagged PDFs) for auditors and regulators
anti_patterns: Editable evidence/workpapers that break immutability and chain of custody
Colour-only risk heatmaps illegible to colour-blind users
Burying the regulatory deadline clock so reporting slips
Letting overdue remediations disappear instead of escalating
Jargon-laden control tasks dumped on non-expert owners
Allowing the same person to operate and independently attest a control
Free-text attestations with no required evidence linkage
Exposing sensitive findings broadly instead of least-privilege scoping
